enrichment.short_description: Sonarr is a free, open-source PVR that automatically monitors, downloads, and organizes TV show episodes from Usenet and BitTorrent sources.
enrichment.seo_title: Sonarr Review: Free Open-Source TV Show PVR Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sonarr is a free open-source PVR that auto-downloads, sorts, and renames TV episodes from Usenet and torrents. See features, setup, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sonarr is a free, open-source personal video recorder (PVR) application that automates the discovery, downloading, sorting, and renaming of TV show episodes. It originated as NZBDrone and was rebranded to Sonarr in 2014; it is now maintained by a volunteer community as part of the Servarr family of tools, alongside Radarr (movies), Lidarr (music), and Readarr (books). Sonarr connects to Usenet indexers and BitTorrent trackers to monitor RSS feeds for new episodes of tracked series, then sends matching releases to a configured download client (such as SABnzbd or qBittorrent). It applies customizable quality profiles, handles failed downloads by automatically retrying alternatives, and renames and organizes finished files into a structured media library, commonly used alongside Plex, Jellyfin, or Emby. It runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, Docker, and NAS platforms like Synology and QNAP. There is no company behind Sonarr and no paid tier; it is entirely free to self-host, with support available through Discord, GitHub, community forums, and a subreddit. It is best suited to technically comfortable users building a self-hosted home media automation stack rather than casual streaming consumers, since it requires pairing with separate indexers and download clients to function.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Sonarr
paragraphs: Sonarr is an open-source PVR (personal video recorder) designed specifically for TV series. Instead of manually searching for and downloading new episodes, Sonarr watches configured indexers for releases matching shows in your library and automatically grabs, verifies, renames, and files them away.

The project began life as NZBDrone before being rebranded Sonarr in 2014, and today it is one of the most widely used tools in the self-hosted media automation ecosystem, often deployed alongside Radarr, Prowlarr, and a media server like Plex or Jellyfin.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Sonarr's core strength is automation: it monitors RSS feeds from Usenet and torrent indexers, applies user-defined quality profiles to decide which release to grab, and automatically retries when a download fails or is later found to be corrupt or fake.

Beyond the grab-and-organize workflow, Sonarr offers a calendar view of upcoming episodes, flexible library views, notification integrations with services like Discord, Slack, and Telegram, and a built-in updater, all managed through a web-based interface accessible from any device on the network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sonarr is completely free and open source under a permissive license, with no premium tier, subscription, or paywalled feature set. Users self-host the application on their own hardware, NAS device, or Docker environment.

Because there is no commercial entity behind Sonarr, support is community-driven rather than vendor-provided, delivered through Discord, IRC, the official forums, GitHub issues, and an active subreddit.

features.title: RSS Monitoring and Auto-Grab
features.description: Continuously scans configured Usenet and torrent indexers for new episodes of tracked shows and automatically sends matches to a download client.

features.title: Customizable Quality Profiles
features.description: Lets users define preferred resolutions, codecs, and release groups so Sonarr always grabs the best available match.

features.title: Automatic Failed Download Handling
features.description: Detects stalled or corrupt downloads and blocks the release, retrying with the next best alternative automatically.

features.title: Calendar View
features.description: Displays upcoming episode air dates and download status across the entire tracked library in one place.

features.title: Metadata and Renaming
features.description: Automatically renames and organizes downloaded files into a consistent folder and naming structure for media servers.

features.title: Download Client Integration
features.description: Works with popular Usenet clients like SABnzbd and torrent clients like qBittorrent and Deluge.

features.title: Notification Integrations
features.description: Sends alerts to Discord, Slack, Telegram, email, and other services when episodes are grabbed or imported.

features.title: Docker and NAS Support
features.description: Runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux, plus Docker containers and NAS platforms like Synology and QNAP.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tier
Deep integration with popular indexers, download clients, and media servers
Highly configurable quality and naming rules
Active, long-running community and frequent updates
Runs on almost any platform, including Docker and NAS devices
cons: Requires pairing with separate indexers and download clients, adding setup complexity
No official cloud-hosted version; self-hosting only
Steep learning curve for non-technical users
No native mobile app for direct control, relying on the web UI or third-party apps
Users are responsible for ensuring their indexer and content sources comply with local law

faqs.question: What is Sonarr?
faqs.answer: Sonarr is a free, open-source PVR that automates finding, downloading, and organizing TV show episodes from Usenet and BitTorrent sources.

faqs.question: Is Sonarr free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sonarr is entirely free and open source, with no paid tier or subscription.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Sonarr support?
faqs.answer: Sonarr runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, Docker, and NAS platforms such as Synology and QNAP.

faqs.question: Does Sonarr download files itself?
faqs.answer: No. Sonarr searches and sends releases to a separate download client, such as SABnzbd or qBittorrent, which performs the actual download.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Sonarr and Radarr?
faqs.answer: Sonarr manages TV series while Radarr, a related project from the same community, manages movies. Both share a similar interface and workflow.

faqs.question: Can I use Sonarr with Plex or Jellyfin?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sonarr is commonly paired with Plex, Jellyfin, or Emby, since it organizes and renames files in a structure those media servers can read.

faqs.question: Does Sonarr require Usenet or torrent access?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sonarr needs at least one configured indexer (Usenet or torrent) and a corresponding download client to function.

faqs.question: Is Sonarr legal to use?
faqs.answer: Sonarr itself is legal open-source software for organizing and automating media downloads. Legality depends on the indexers and content users configure it to access, and users are responsible for complying with applicable laws.

use_cases.title: Home media server automation
use_cases.description: Automatically builds and maintains a complete, well-organized TV library for a home Plex or Jellyfin server.
use_cases.audience: Home theater and self-hosted media enthusiasts

use_cases.title: Multi-show episode tracking
use_cases.description: Tracks dozens or hundreds of ongoing series simultaneously without manual checking.
use_cases.audience: TV viewers managing large watch libraries

use_cases.title: Self-hosted NAS media management
use_cases.description: Runs as a lightweight background service on NAS hardware to keep a media library current.

features: Community support via Discord, forums, and GitHub

enrichment.short_description: Sonatype Nexus Repository is an artifact and binary repository manager that lets development teams store, secure, and distribute software components and containers across the build pipeline.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community Edition); Pro pricing available on request
enrichment.seo_title: Sonatype Nexus Repository: Features, Pricing and Review
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sonatype Nexus Repository manages build artifacts and containers across Maven, npm, Docker, PyPI, and more. Compare Community vs Pro editions and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sonatype Nexus Repository is a universal artifact repository manager used by software teams to store, proxy, secure, and distribute build outputs such as libraries, containers, and AI or ML models across the software development lifecycle. Built by Sonatype, a company founded in 2008 and headquartered in Fulton, Maryland, with roughly 500 to 1,000 employees, Nexus Repository is trusted by a large share of Fortune 100 companies and supports more than 20 package formats, including Maven, npm, Docker, PyPI, NuGet, RubyGems, Helm, Cargo, Conda, and Hugging Face models. It is offered in two editions: a free Community Edition aimed at small teams needing core repository management, and a paid Pro edition adding enterprise features such as single sign-on and SAML authentication, high availability, disaster recovery, replication, and integration with Sonatype Repository Firewall for malware and open-source risk detection. Deployment options include self-hosted (on-premises or private cloud), fully managed SaaS, and air-gapped disconnected environments for high-security use cases. Pro pricing is not published and requires contacting Sonatype's sales team; it typically scales with usage and organization size. Nexus Repository is aimed primarily at DevOps, platform engineering, and security teams that need a centralized, auditable source of truth for build artifacts and container images feeding CI/CD pipelines.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Sonatype Nexus Repository
paragraphs: Sonatype Nexus Repository is a binary and artifact repository manager that acts as the single source of truth for everything a software build produces or consumes, from third-party libraries pulled from public registries to internally built packages and container images.

It is developed by Sonatype, a DevSecOps and software supply chain security company founded in 2008 and headquartered in Fulton, Maryland, which also builds the related Sonatype Repository Firewall and Sonatype Lifecycle products for open-source risk management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Nexus Repository supports more than 20 package formats in a single server, including Maven, npm, Docker, PyPI, NuGet, RubyGems, Helm charts, Cargo, Conan, Composer, Conda, Yum, and Hugging Face models, so organizations can consolidate multiple format-specific registries into one platform.

Enterprise-focused capabilities include role-based access control, TLS encryption, SAML and SSO support, proxy repositories with local caching that can dramatically cut build times, complete audit trails of component origin and publication, and integration with Repository Firewall for automated malware and vulnerability alerts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Nexus Repository is available as a free Community Edition, which covers core artifact storage, proxying, and hosting for smaller teams, and as a paid Pro edition that unlocks high availability, disaster recovery, replication, and advanced security integrations.

Sonatype does not publish Pro pricing on its website; prospective customers must request a quote, and cost typically depends on deployment size, number of repository formats used, and whether the customer chooses self-hosted, SaaS, or air-gapped deployment.

features.title: Universal Format Support
features.description: Manages more than 20 package and container formats, including Maven, npm, Docker, PyPI, NuGet, and Helm, from a single server.

features.title: Proxy Repositories and Local Caching
features.description: Caches remote dependencies locally to cut build latency and reduce reliance on public registries.

features.title: Role-Based Access Control
features.description: Fine-grained permissions control who can read, write, or administer specific repositories and formats.

features.title: High Availability and Disaster Recovery
features.description: Pro-tier clustering and replication keep repositories available and resilient for large organizations.

features.title: Audit Trails and Component Metadata
features.description: Tracks the origin, publisher, and history of every artifact for compliance and traceability.

features.title: Repository Firewall Integration
features.description: Flags malware and open-source risk in incoming components before they enter the build pipeline.

features.title: REST API and CI/CD Integrations
features.description: Connects to Jenkins, GitHub Actions, and other pipeline tools via a documented REST API.

features.title: Flexible Deployment Options
features.description: Available as SaaS, self-hosted, or fully air-gapped for regulated and high-security environments.

pros: Broad support for more than 20 package and container formats in one platform
Trusted by a large share of Fortune 100 companies
Strong security and compliance integrations via Repository Firewall
Flexible deployment: SaaS, self-hosted, or air-gapped
Free Community Edition available for smaller teams
cons: Pro pricing is not publicly listed and requires a sales conversation
Advanced features like high availability are gated behind the paid Pro tier
Can be resource-intensive to self-host at large scale
Initial configuration and format setup can be complex for smaller teams

faqs.question: What is Sonatype Nexus Repository used for?
faqs.answer: It is used to store, proxy, secure, and distribute software build artifacts, dependencies, and container images across the development pipeline.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of Nexus Repository?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community Edition is free and provides core repository management for smaller teams.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Nexus Repository Community and Pro?
faqs.answer: Pro adds enterprise features such as SSO/SAML, high availability, disaster recovery, replication, and Repository Firewall integration that are not in the free Community Edition.

faqs.question: What package formats does Nexus Repository support?
faqs.answer: It supports more than 20 formats, including Maven, npm, Docker, PyPI, NuGet, RubyGems, Helm, Cargo, Conda, Composer, and Hugging Face models.

faqs.question: Can Nexus Repository be hosted in the cloud?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sonatype offers a fully managed SaaS deployment in addition to self-hosted and air-gapped options.

faqs.question: Does Sonatype publish Nexus Repository Pro pricing?
faqs.answer: No, Pro pricing is custom and requires contacting Sonatype's sales team for a quote.

faqs.question: Who owns Sonatype?
faqs.answer: Sonatype has been backed by Vista Equity Partners since a 2019 investment.

faqs.question: Does Nexus Repository integrate with CI/CD tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, it exposes a REST API and integrates with common CI/CD tools such as Jenkins and GitHub Actions.

use_cases.title: Centralized artifact management for enterprise builds
use_cases.description: Consolidates dependencies, internal packages, and container images into one governed repository.

use_cases.title: Secure software supply chain management
use_cases.description: Proxies and audits third-party components to reduce open-source risk and malware exposure.

use_cases.title: Container and Kubernetes image registry
use_cases.description: Serves as a private Docker registry for teams running containerized workloads.
use_cases.audience: Engineering teams running Docker and Kubernetes

enrichment.short_description: Sorted 3 (Sorted3) is a Mac and iOS productivity app that merges tasks, calendar events, and notes into a single hyper-scheduled timeline.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Pro unlock from $14.99 one-time on iOS)
enrichment.seo_title: Sorted 3 Review: Mac and iOS Task and Calendar App
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sorted 3 merges tasks, calendar events, and notes into one timeline for Mac and iOS. See features, pricing, and how Sorted3 compares to other planners.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sorted 3, also styled Sorted3, is a task, calendar, and notes app for macOS and iOS made by StaySorted Limited, a company founded in 2015 and based in Hong Kong. Its core idea is a unified timeline that combines to-do tasks, calendar events, and richly formatted notes into one hyper-scheduled daily view, so users can drag and reorder their day using a feature called the Time Ruler rather than juggling separate apps. Other notable features include Magic Select for bulk editing multiple items at once, natural language input for fast task entry, iCloud sync across iPhone, iPad, and Mac, home screen and lock screen widgets, customizable repeat rules, light and dark themes, a URL scheme for automation, and Siri integration. Sorted 3 follows a freemium model: a free tier covers the core timeline and basic scheduling, while a Pro unlock removes limits and adds Magic Select, advanced repeat rules, and widgets, priced as a one-time purchase of $14.99 on iOS and $24.99 on Mac. The app is Apple-ecosystem only, with no Android, Windows, or web version, and has earned a 4.8-star App Store rating from more than 21,000 reviews plus recognition as an Apple App of the Day in over 130 regions. It suits individuals who want time-blocking style planning tightly integrated with Apple devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Sorted 3
paragraphs: Sorted 3 is a personal productivity app built around a single unified timeline that blends tasks, calendar events, and notes, letting users see and rearrange their entire day in one visual space instead of switching between a calendar app and a separate to-do list.
It is developed by StaySorted Limited, a Hong Kong-based studio founded in 2015, and is available on iPhone, iPad, and Mac with iCloud sync keeping the timeline consistent across devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The signature Time Ruler lets users drag tasks and events to reshape their schedule with a few taps, while Magic Select enables bulk actions like rescheduling or tagging several items at once. Natural language input allows quick task creation by typing phrases like 'lunch with Sam tomorrow at noon' directly into the timeline.

Additional features include home and lock screen widgets, powerful custom repeat rules for recurring tasks, light and dark themes, a URL scheme for automation with tools like Shortcuts, and Siri integration for voice-based task capture.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sorted 3 uses a freemium model: the free version includes the core unified timeline, basic scheduling, and iCloud sync, which is enough for lightweight personal use.
Unlocking the full Pro feature set, including Magic Select, advanced repeat rules, and widgets, is a one-time purchase rather than a recurring subscription, priced at $14.99 on iOS and $24.99 on Mac.

features.title: Unified Timeline
features.description: Combines tasks, calendar events, and notes into a single scrollable daily and weekly view.

features.title: Time Ruler
features.description: A drag-and-drop scheduling tool for quickly reshaping the day's plan.

features.title: Magic Select
features.description: Lets users select and bulk-edit multiple tasks or events at once.

features.title: Natural Language Input
features.description: Creates tasks and events from plain-language phrases without manual field entry.

features.title: iCloud Sync
features.description: Keeps the timeline consistent in real time across iPhone, iPad, and Mac.

features.title: Widgets
features.description: Home screen and lock screen widgets on iOS and macOS for at-a-glance scheduling.

features.title: Custom Repeat Rules
features.description: Supports flexible, powerful recurrence patterns for recurring tasks and events.

features.title: Siri and URL Scheme Integration
features.description: Enables voice task capture and automation with Shortcuts and other Apple tools.

pros: Clean, unified timeline reduces app-switching between calendar and to-do list
Deep Apple ecosystem integration, including widgets and Siri
One-time Pro purchase option instead of a forced subscription
Fast natural language task entry
Highly rated on the App Store with over 21,000 reviews
cons: Apple ecosystem only, no Android, Windows, or web version
No standalone web app for browser-based access
Some advanced features are locked behind the Pro unlock
Small independent team means slower feature velocity than larger competitors like Todoist

faqs.question: What is Sorted 3?
faqs.answer: Sorted 3 (Sorted3) is a Mac and iOS app that combines tasks, calendar events, and notes into one unified, drag-and-drop timeline.

faqs.question: Is Sorted 3 free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free version is available with core timeline and scheduling features; a paid Pro unlock adds advanced tools.

faqs.question: What platforms does Sorted 3 support?
faqs.answer: Sorted 3 is available on iPhone, iPad, and Mac, with iCloud sync across devices.

faqs.question: Who makes Sorted 3?
faqs.answer: Sorted 3 is developed by StaySorted Limited, founded in 2015 and based in Hong Kong.

faqs.question: Is there an Android version of Sorted 3?
faqs.answer: No, Sorted 3 is exclusive to Apple platforms and has no Android release.

faqs.question: Does Sorted 3 sync across devices?
faqs.answer: Yes, it uses iCloud sync to keep the timeline current across iPhone, iPad, and Mac in real time.

faqs.question: What is the Time Ruler feature?
faqs.answer: The Time Ruler is Sorted 3's drag-and-drop tool for quickly rearranging tasks and events within the day.

faqs.question: Is Sorted 3 a one-time purchase or subscription?
faqs.answer: The Pro unlock is a one-time purchase, priced at $14.99 on iOS and $24.99 on Mac, rather than a recurring subscription.

use_cases.title: Daily time-blocking for professionals

use_cases.title: Combining notes and to-dos in one place
use_cases.description: Keeps richly formatted notes next to related tasks and events instead of in a separate notes app.

use_cases.title: Cross-device planning across Apple devices

enrichment.short_description: SourceBans++ is a free, source-available admin and ban management system for Source-engine game servers such as Counter-Strike, Team Fortress 2, and Garrys Mod.
enrichment.seo_title: SourceBans++ Review: Free Ban Management for Source Servers
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SourceBans++ is a free admin and ban management tool for Source-engine game servers like CS, TF2, and Garrys Mod. See features and setup requirements.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SourceBans++ is a free, source-available admin, ban, and communications management system built for game servers running on Valve's Source and Source 2 engines, including Counter-Strike (Source, Global Offensive, and 2), Team Fortress 2, Garrys Mod, Day of Defeat: Source, Left 4 Dead and Left 4 Dead 2, Insurgency, and other SourceMod-compatible titles. It is an enhanced, community-maintained fork of the original SourceBans project and has been actively supported since 2015. The system has two connected halves: a PHP and MySQL web admin panel where server administrators manage bans, mutes, server access, and admin permissions, and a set of SourceMod plugins running on each game server that enforce those decisions in-game and report new admin actions back to the panel in real time, so a ban applied on one server can propagate instantly across every connected server. SourceBans++ is maintained by an open-source development team (github.com/sbpp) with more than 2,000 servers reported to run it. Self-hosting is, and will remain, completely free; a separate commercial managed-hosting alternative called RookHelm exists for operators who prefer not to self-host. There is no company, founding date, or headquarters associated with the project itself, as it is a community-driven open-source tool rather than a commercial product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is SourceBans++
paragraphs: SourceBans++ is an admin and ban-management platform purpose-built for Source-engine game servers. It gives community and clan server operators a centralized web panel to manage bans, mutes, and admin permissions across every server they run.

The project is a community-maintained, source-available fork of the original SourceBans, actively supported since 2015 and still developed on GitHub, with a large install base reported at over 2,000 servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The system pairs a PHP and MySQL web panel with SourceMod plugins installed on each game server. When an admin issues a ban, mute, or other action from the panel or in-game, it is enforced immediately and synced in real time to every connected server.

SourceBans++ supports a wide range of Source and Source 2 titles with a SourceMod release, including Counter-Strike, Team Fortress 2, Garrys Mod, Left 4 Dead 1 and 2, Day of Defeat: Source, Insurgency, and NMRiH, making it a common choice for multi-server communities running mixed game rosters.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SourceBans++ is free to self-host and always will be, according to the project's own documentation, with no license fee for the software itself.

For operators who would rather not manage the PHP, MySQL, and SourceMod setup themselves, a separate commercial managed-hosting service called RookHelm is available as a paid alternative, though it is a third-party offering rather than an official SourceBans++ product.

features.title: Web Admin Panel
features.description: Centralized PHP-based panel for managing bans, mutes, server access, and admin permissions.

features.title: SourceMod Plugin Integration
features.description: In-game plugins enforce panel decisions directly on the game server.

features.description: Bans and mutes issued on one server sync instantly across all connected servers.

features.title: Admin Permission Management
features.description: Fine-grained control over which admins can take which actions on which servers.

features.title: Multi-Server Support
features.description: Manages an unlimited number of connected game servers from a single panel.

features.title: Ban History and Comms Logging
features.description: Keeps a searchable record of past bans, mutes, and admin actions.

features.title: Broad Game Support
features.description: Works with Counter-Strike, Team Fortress 2, Garrys Mod, Left 4 Dead, Insurgency, and other SourceMod-compatible titles.

features.title: Self-Hosted, Free Deployment
features.description: Runs entirely on infrastructure the operator controls, with no licensing cost.

pros: Completely free to self-host with no license fee
Wide support across Source and Source 2 engine games
Real-time ban and mute sync across multiple servers
Actively maintained on GitHub since 2015
Extensible through the broader SourceMod plugin ecosystem
cons: Requires manual PHP, MySQL, and SourceMod setup and maintenance
No official managed hosting from the project itself, only third-party options
Web interface is dated compared to modern SaaS admin panels
Support is community-based through forums rather than a dedicated vendor

faqs.question: What is SourceBans++?
faqs.answer: SourceBans++ is a free, source-available web panel and SourceMod plugin suite for managing bans, mutes, and admin permissions on Source-engine game servers.

faqs.question: Is SourceBans++ free?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting SourceBans++ is completely free and, according to the project, will remain that way.

faqs.question: What games does SourceBans++ support?
faqs.answer: It supports Source and Source 2 titles with a SourceMod release, including Counter-Strike, Team Fortress 2, Garrys Mod, Left 4 Dead, Day of Defeat: Source, and Insurgency.

faqs.question: What do I need to install SourceBans++?
faqs.answer: A web server with PHP, a MySQL database, and SourceMod installed on each connected game server.

faqs.question: What is the difference between SourceBans and SourceBans++?
faqs.answer: SourceBans++ is a community-maintained, actively developed fork of the original SourceBans project, adding fixes and features since 2015.

faqs.question: Does SourceBans++ sync bans across multiple servers?
faqs.answer: Yes, bans and mutes issued on one connected server propagate in real time to all other connected servers.

faqs.question: Is there managed hosting for SourceBans++?
faqs.answer: The core project is self-hosted only; a third-party service called RookHelm offers commercial managed hosting as an alternative.

faqs.question: Does SourceBans++ require SourceMod?
faqs.answer: Yes, SourceMod must be installed on each game server so the SourceBans++ plugins can enforce bans and mutes in-game.

use_cases.audience: Source-engine game server hosts and network admins

enrichment.short_description: Sourcegraph Cody is an enterprise AI coding assistant that uses Sourcegraph's code graph to give large language models deep context from an organizations entire remote codebase.
enrichment.seo_title: Sourcegraph Cody Review: Enterprise AI Coding Assistant
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sourcegraph Cody is an enterprise AI coding assistant with full codebase context. See features, pricing, and how it compares to Copilot and Cursor.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sourcegraph Cody is an AI coding assistant built by Sourcegraph, a code intelligence company founded in 2013 and headquartered in San Francisco with an estimated 100 to 250 employees. Cody's differentiator is context: rather than relying only on locally open files like many editor-based AI assistants, it uses Sourcegraph's code search and graph engine to pull relevant functions, types, and usage patterns from across an organization's entire remote codebase, including large multi-repository enterprise monorepos with millions of lines of code. Features include an AI chat interface for asking questions about code, generating and editing code, auto-edit contextual suggestions based on cursor position, customizable shared prompts for common team workflows, and support in VS Code, JetBrains IDEs, an experimental Visual Studio integration, the Sourcegraph web app, and a command-line interface, with code host integrations for GitHub and GitLab. As of mid-2025, Sourcegraph discontinued the self-serve Cody Free and Cody Pro plans; Cody is now available exclusively as part of a Sourcegraph Enterprise subscription, which is sold on a custom-quoted annual contract reported to start in the tens of thousands of dollars and has previously been reported at roughly $59 per user per month. This makes Cody best suited to larger engineering organizations already using or considering Sourcegraph Enterprise rather than individual developers or small teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Sourcegraph Cody
paragraphs: Sourcegraph Cody is an AI coding assistant from Sourcegraph, a San Francisco company founded in 2013 that built its reputation on large-scale code search and code intelligence for enterprise engineering teams.

Cody's core idea is codebase-aware AI: it pairs a large language model with Sourcegraph's existing search and code graph so that answers, generated code, and suggestions are grounded in an organization's real, current codebase rather than only the file currently open in an editor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cody offers an AI chat interface for asking questions about unfamiliar code, generating new code, and requesting edits, plus auto-edit suggestions that adapt to cursor position and recent changes. Teams can create and share customizable prompts for repeatable workflows like writing tests or documentation.

Because it draws context from Sourcegraph's search index, Cody can reference relevant functions, types, and patterns from across many repositories at once, which is particularly useful in large enterprise monorepos where a single file rarely tells the whole story. It is available in VS Code, JetBrains IDEs, an experimental Visual Studio integration, the Sourcegraph web app, and a CLI, and integrates with GitHub and GitLab code hosts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: As of mid-2025, Sourcegraph stopped accepting new sign-ups for the self-serve Cody Free and Cody Pro plans and terminated existing accounts, folding Cody entirely into Sourcegraph Enterprise.

Sourcegraph Enterprise is sold on a custom-quoted annual contract; its public pricing page lists a starting reference figure in the tens of thousands of dollars for larger deployments, while Cody-specific access has also been reported at around $59 per user per month under earlier enterprise contract terms. Exact pricing depends on team size, deployment model, and AI usage volume, and requires contacting Sourcegraph's sales team.

features.title: Codebase-Wide Context
features.description: Pulls relevant code, types, and usage patterns from an organization's entire remote codebase via Sourcegraph's search engine.

features.title: AI Chat for Code Understanding and Generation
features.description: Lets developers ask questions, generate code, and request edits in natural language.

features.title: Auto-Edit Contextual Suggestions
features.description: Offers inline code suggestions based on cursor position and recent edits.

features.title: Customizable Shared Prompts
features.description: Teams can build and share reusable prompts for common coding and documentation tasks.

features.title: Multi-IDE Support
features.description: Available in VS Code, JetBrains IDEs, and an experimental Visual Studio integration.

features.title: Large Monorepo Awareness
features.description: Designed to stay useful in enterprise codebases spanning millions of lines across many repositories.

features.title: CLI and Web App Access
features.description: Usable outside the IDE via a command-line interface and the Sourcegraph web application.

faqs.question: What is Sourcegraph Cody?
faqs.answer: Cody is Sourcegraph's AI coding assistant, which uses Sourcegraph's code search and graph engine to give LLMs context from an organization's entire remote codebase.

faqs.question: Is Cody free to use?
faqs.answer: No longer as a standalone plan. Sourcegraph discontinued the self-serve Cody Free and Cody Pro tiers in mid-2025; Cody is now included only with Sourcegraph Enterprise.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Cody and GitHub Copilot?
faqs.answer: Copilot primarily uses local file context, while Cody pulls context from an organization's full remote codebase across many repositories via Sourcegraph's search index.

faqs.question: What IDEs does Cody support?
faqs.answer: Cody is available in VS Code, JetBrains IDEs, an experimental Visual Studio integration, the Sourcegraph web app, and a CLI.

faqs.question: Does Cody require a full Sourcegraph Enterprise license?
faqs.answer: Yes, as of mid-2025 Cody is only accessible through a Sourcegraph Enterprise subscription.

faqs.question: How does Cody get codebase context?
faqs.answer: It uses Sourcegraph's existing code search and graph engine to retrieve relevant functions, types, and usage patterns from across an organization's repositories.

faqs.question: Who founded Sourcegraph?
faqs.answer: Sourcegraph was founded in 2013 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Can individual developers still sign up for Cody?
faqs.answer: No, self-serve individual plans were discontinued in mid-2025; Cody now requires an organization's Sourcegraph Enterprise contract.

enrichment.short_description: SourceHut is an independent, privacy-focused software forge offering Git and Mercurial hosting, bug tracking, continuous integration, and mailing lists without ads or tracking.
enrichment.seo_title: SourceHut Review: Privacy-First Git Hosting and Forge
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SourceHut is an independent, ad-free software forge with Git/Mercurial hosting, CI, bug tracking, and mailing lists. See features and pay-what-you-can pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SourceHut is an independent software forge created by developer Drew DeVault, launched to public alpha on November 15, 2018, as a minimalist, privacy-first alternative to platforms like GitHub and GitLab. It bundles Git and Mercurial repository hosting with continuous integration (builds.sr.ht), mailing-list-based code review and discussion (lists.sr.ht), and issue tracking (via its built-in tracker) into one connected suite of tools. SourceHut's stated principles include no tracking or advertising, full functionality without JavaScript, and being 100 percent open-source software that anyone can self-host. Its pricing model is unusual: rather than differentiating features by tier, all paid subscriptions unlock the same feature set, and users choose a monthly contribution of $4, $8, or $12 (or the euro equivalent) according to their means, with a discount for annual billing and financial aid available on request. Payment is only required to host one's own projects; creating an account to contribute to existing projects hosted on SourceHut is free. As a solo-founder-led, independent project rather than a venture-backed company, SourceHut does not publish employee counts or a formal headquarters. It is most popular with open-source maintainers and developers who prefer a mailing-list-centric, Linux-kernel-style collaboration workflow over pull requests, and who value privacy and minimalism over a polished modern interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is SourceHut
paragraphs: SourceHut is a software forge built and maintained by developer Drew DeVault, launched to public alpha in November 2018 as a deliberately minimal, privacy-respecting alternative to mainstream platforms like GitHub and GitLab.

Rather than a single monolithic product, SourceHut is a suite of independent tools, including git.sr.ht and hg.sr.ht for repository hosting, builds.sr.ht for continuous integration, lists.sr.ht for mailing lists, and SourceHut's built-in issue tracker, that interoperate but can also be used individually.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: SourceHut emphasizes privacy and simplicity: the platform states it has no tracking or advertising, and all core features work without JavaScript, appealing to developers who want a lightweight, fast interface.

The platform is 100 percent open source and self-hostable, and favors a mailing-list-based code review workflow reminiscent of projects like the Linux kernel, alongside built-in CI and issue tracking that many competing forges only offer through third-party add-ons.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SourceHut uses a pay-according-to-your-means model with three tiers, Amateur Hackers, Typical Hackers, and Professional Hackers, priced at $4, $8, and $12 per month respectively (or the euro equivalent), with no functional difference between them beyond the contribution amount.

Paying annually gives roughly two months free compared to monthly billing, and users experiencing financial hardship can request aid; contributing to projects already hosted on SourceHut does not require a paid account at all.

features.description: lists.sr.ht supports a mailing-list-based patch and discussion workflow favored by many established open-source projects.

features.title: Issue and Bug Tracking
features.description: SourceHut's built-in issue tracker provides lightweight tracking integrated with the rest of the suite.

features.title: No Tracking or Advertising
features.description: The platform states it does not track users or display ads anywhere in its products.

features.title: Works Without JavaScript
features.description: All core features are accessible even with JavaScript disabled, keeping pages fast and lightweight.

features.title: Fully Open-Source and Self-Hostable
features.description: The entire platform's source code is open, so organizations can self-host their own instance.

features.title: Pay-What-You-Can Pricing Tiers
features.description: Subscribers choose a monthly contribution level rather than being gated by a feature-based tier.

pros: Strong privacy stance with no tracking or advertising
Fully open source and self-hostable
Works without JavaScript for a fast, lightweight experience
Affordable pay-what-you-can pricing with financial aid available
Built-in CI and mailing-list workflows favored by many established open-source projects
cons: Less polished, modern interface than GitHub or GitLab
Smaller community and ecosystem than mainstream forges
Mailing-list-centric workflow has a learning curve for developers used to pull requests
Fewer built-in project management features compared to GitHub

faqs.question: What is SourceHut?
faqs.answer: SourceHut is an independent, privacy-focused software forge offering Git and Mercurial hosting, continuous integration, mailing lists, and issue tracking.

faqs.question: Who created SourceHut?
faqs.answer: SourceHut was created by developer Drew DeVault and launched to public alpha in November 2018.

faqs.question: How much does SourceHut cost?
faqs.answer: Paid tiers are $4, $8, or $12 per month (or the euro equivalent), chosen according to what the user can afford, with all tiers offering the same features.

faqs.question: Is SourceHut free to use?
faqs.answer: Creating an account to contribute to existing projects is free; paying is only required to host your own projects on SourceHut.

faqs.question: Does SourceHut support pull requests like GitHub?
faqs.answer: SourceHut favors a mailing-list-based patch review workflow rather than GitHub-style pull requests, though it can be adapted to different workflows.

faqs.question: Can I self-host SourceHut?
faqs.answer: Yes, SourceHut is 100 percent open-source software and can be self-hosted by anyone.

faqs.question: Does SourceHut track users or show ads?
faqs.answer: No, SourceHut states it has no tracking or advertising anywhere on the platform.

faqs.question: What services are included in a SourceHut subscription?
faqs.answer: A subscription covers Git and Mercurial hosting, continuous integration, mailing lists, and issue tracking across the SourceHut suite.

enrichment.short_description: Spark Mail is an AI-powered email client from Readdle that offers a Smart Inbox, team collaboration, and AI writing and meeting-notes features across desktop and mobile.
enrichment.seo_title: Spark Mail Review: AI Email Client Features and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Spark Mail is an AI-powered email app with Smart Inbox and team features from Readdle. Compare Free, Plus, Pro, and Enterprise pricing plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Spark Mail is an email client built by Readdle, a productivity software company founded in 2007 in Odesa, Ukraine, which also makes PDF Expert, Scanner Pro, Documents, and Calendars. Spark itself launched in 2015 and has since evolved into an AI-assisted email app centered on a Smart Inbox that automatically prioritizes important messages, alongside Spark plus AI features such as an AI writing assistant and AI-generated meeting notes. It is available across iOS, Android, Mac, Windows, and the web, and supports team use cases like shared drafts, delegation, comments, and custom email templates in addition to personal email. Pricing follows a freemium model with four tiers: a Free plan with unlimited accounts and a Smart Inbox; Plus at roughly EUR10 per user per month (about EUR8.25 per month billed annually) adding the AI writing assistant and 40 AI meeting notes monthly; Pro at roughly EUR22 per user per month (about EUR19.08 per month billed annually) adding unlimited AI meeting notes, read statuses, HubSpot integration, and a command-line tool; and a custom-priced Enterprise plan adding advanced security controls and a dedicated success manager. All paid plans include a 7-day free trial. Readdle now employs several hundred people across more than 30 locations, including hubs in Odesa, Kyiv, London, Warsaw, and Berlin, making Spark best suited to individuals and small-to-midsize teams that want AI assistance layered directly into a familiar email workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Spark Mail
paragraphs: Spark Mail is an email client developed by Readdle, a Ukrainian productivity software company founded in 2007 and headquartered in Odesa. Spark launched in 2015 and has grown into one of the most popular alternative mail apps for both individuals and teams.

Its central feature is a Smart Inbox that automatically separates important personal messages from newsletters and notifications, combined more recently with Spark plus AI tools for writing assistance and meeting notes, aimed at reducing time spent triaging email.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Beyond the Smart Inbox, Spark offers an AI writing assistant to draft and refine emails, AI-generated meeting notes, custom email templates, and productivity integrations with calendars and tools like HubSpot.

For teams, Spark adds collaborative features such as shared drafts, delegated inboxes, internal comments on emails, and, on the Pro plan, a command-line interface for power users, all synced across iOS, Android, Mac, Windows, and web apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Spark's Free plan covers unlimited email accounts, the Smart Inbox, notifications, and calendar access at no cost, making it usable as a full-featured everyday email client without payment.

Paid tiers are priced per user: Plus adds AI writing and a monthly quota of AI meeting notes, Pro adds unlimited AI meeting notes and deeper team and integration features, and Enterprise offers custom pricing with advanced security and dedicated support. All paid plans include a 7-day free trial, and annual billing offers roughly a 13 percent discount over monthly pricing.

faqs.question: What is Spark Mail?
faqs.answer: Spark Mail is an AI-assisted email client with a Smart Inbox, team collaboration tools, and AI writing and meeting-notes features.

faqs.question: Who makes Spark Mail?
faqs.answer: Spark Mail is developed by Readdle, a productivity software company founded in 2007 in Odesa, Ukraine.

faqs.question: Is Spark Mail free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a Free plan is available with unlimited email accounts and the Smart Inbox; AI features and advanced team tools require a paid plan.

faqs.question: What is included in Spark plus AI?
faqs.answer: Spark plus AI includes an AI writing assistant and AI-generated meeting notes, with the number of monthly notes varying by plan.

faqs.question: Does Spark Mail support team email collaboration?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans include shared drafts, delegation, internal comments, and other team collaboration tools.

faqs.question: What platforms does Spark Mail run on?
faqs.answer: Spark Mail is available on iOS, Android, Mac, Windows, and the web.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Spark Plus and Spark Pro?
faqs.answer: Plus includes limited monthly AI meeting notes and essential team features, while Pro adds unlimited AI meeting notes, read statuses, HubSpot integration, and Spark CLI.

faqs.question: Does Spark Mail offer an Enterprise plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Enterprise offers custom pricing with advanced security controls and a dedicated success manager.

enrichment.short_description: Speakeasy is a developer platform that automatically generates type-safe SDKs, Terraform providers, and other client libraries from an API OpenAPI specification.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Business plan from $600/month per language, billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: Speakeasy Review: API and SDK Generation Platform Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Speakeasy auto-generates type-safe SDKs, MCP servers, and Terraform providers from OpenAPI specs. Compare Free, Business, and Enterprise pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Speakeasy is a developer tools company founded in 2022 in San Francisco by ex-Apollo GraphQL engineers, including CEO Sagar Batchu, that automates the generation of production-quality, idiomatic SDKs, Terraform providers, and MCP servers directly from an API's OpenAPI specification. Rather than producing thin, auto-generated API wrappers, Speakeasy aims to output SDKs that read like hand-written client libraries, supporting multiple languages such as TypeScript, Python, Go, and Java, with automatic publishing to package managers and generated documentation sites. It also supports newer needs like generating Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers so APIs can be exposed to AI agents, plus an OpenAPI toolchain for linting and validating specs. Pricing follows a freemium model: a Free tier includes SDK and MCP generation with a license for one language and 250 operations per language; a Business tier adds OAuth support, webhooks, server-sent events, and docsite integration for $720 per month per licensed language, discounted to $600 per month when billed annually; and a custom-priced Enterprise tier adds SSO, audit logs, SLAs, and concierge onboarding. Speakeasy raised a Series A round of more than $20 million led by Andreessen Horowitz with participation from FirstMark Capital, following an earlier $7.6 million seed round led by GV. Users retain full ownership of the code Speakeasy generates, even if they later stop using the service, making it best suited to API-first companies that need to publish and maintain official SDKs across multiple languages.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Speakeasy
paragraphs: Speakeasy is a developer platform, founded in 2022 in San Francisco, that turns an API's OpenAPI specification into production-ready, idiomatic SDKs, Terraform providers, and other client libraries, rather than the thin auto-generated wrappers produced by many older code generation tools.

The company was started by former Apollo GraphQL engineers, including CEO Sagar Batchu, and has raised a Series A round of more than $20 million led by Andreessen Horowitz, building on an earlier seed round led by GV.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Speakeasy generates SDKs in multiple languages, including TypeScript, Python, Go, and Java, and can automatically publish them to the relevant package manager while generating accompanying documentation sites, so API providers can ship official client libraries without maintaining generator code by hand.

The platform also supports newer developer needs, including generating Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers that expose an API to AI agents, an OpenAPI toolchain for linting and validating specs, and support for OAuth, webhooks, and server-sent events on higher-tier plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Speakeasy's Free tier includes SDK and MCP generation with a license for one language and a quota of 250 operations per language, along with community Slack support, making it usable for smaller or early-stage APIs at no cost.

The Business tier, priced per licensed language at $720 per month ($600 per month billed annually), adds OAuth support, webhooks, server-sent events, and docsite integration, while a custom-priced Enterprise tier adds SSO, audit logs, SLAs, dedicated support, and concierge onboarding for larger organizations.

features.title: Automatic SDK Generation
features.description: Generates production-quality, idiomatic SDKs directly from an OpenAPI specification.

features.title: Multi-Language Support
features.description: Supports SDK generation for languages including TypeScript, Python, Go, and Java.

features.title: Auto-Publish to Package Managers
features.description: Automatically publishes generated SDKs to the appropriate package registry.

features.title: MCP Server Generation
features.description: Generates Model Context Protocol servers so APIs can be exposed to AI agents.

features.title: OAuth, Webhooks, and SSE Support
features.description: Adds support for OAuth authentication, webhooks, and server-sent events on paid plans.

features.title: Docsite Generation
features.description: Automatically builds and integrates documentation sites alongside generated SDKs.

features.title: Full Code Ownership
features.description: Users retain complete ownership of generated SDK code, even after discontinuing the service.

features.title: OpenAPI Toolchain
features.description: Provides spec linting and validation tools to keep OpenAPI definitions clean and accurate.

pros: Generates production-quality, idiomatic SDKs rather than raw API wrappers
Users retain full ownership of all generated code
Supports modern needs like MCP servers for AI agent integration
Free tier available for smaller projects and early-stage APIs
Backed by well-known investors including Andreessen Horowitz
cons: Business plan pricing is charged per language, which scales quickly for multi-language SDK needs
Advanced features like SSO and audit logs require custom Enterprise pricing
Relatively young company, founded in 2022, compared to established API tooling vendors
Output quality depends on having a well-structured OpenAPI specification

faqs.question: What is Speakeasy?
faqs.answer: Speakeasy is a developer platform that automatically generates type-safe SDKs, Terraform providers, and MCP servers from an API's OpenAPI specification.

faqs.question: Who founded Speakeasy?
faqs.answer: Speakeasy was founded in 2022 in San Francisco by former Apollo GraphQL engineers, including CEO Sagar Batchu.

faqs.question: Is Speakeasy free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, a Free tier is available with a license for one language and 250 operations per language; paid plans unlock more languages and features.

faqs.question: What programming languages does Speakeasy generate SDKs for?
faqs.answer: Speakeasy generates SDKs for multiple languages, including TypeScript, Python, Go, and Java.

faqs.question: Do I own the SDK code Speakeasy generates?
faqs.answer: Yes, users retain full ownership of generated SDK code even if they stop using Speakeasy.

faqs.question: What is Speakeasy's AI control plane?
faqs.answer: It is a related Speakeasy offering focused on connecting, securing, and governing AI agents, including custom MCP server creation and serverless hosting.

faqs.question: How much does Speakeasy Business cost?
faqs.answer: The Business plan costs $720 per month per licensed language, or $600 per month when billed annually.

faqs.question: Does Speakeasy support MCP servers?
faqs.answer: Yes, Speakeasy can generate Model Context Protocol servers to expose an API to AI agents.

use_cases.title: Generating Terraform providers from an API spec
